html:
Carl Peter Thunberg, born on November 11, 1743, in Jönköping, and died on August 8, 1828, at Tunaberg in Uppsala, was a Swedish botanist and zoologist, a student of Carl von Linné. He is remembered as "The Japanese Linné" and the "Father of South African Botany."

==Early Years== Carl Peter Thunberg was the son of Jean Thunberg, an accountant, and Margareta Starkman. In 1784, he married Brita Charlotta Ruda, who died childless in 1814. From Jönköping, Thunberg traveled to Uppsala University, where he studied natural philosophy and medicine as a student of Carl von Linné, earning his preliminary degree in 1767. Through recommendations from Linné, Thunberg received a scholarship to study in Amsterdam and Paris. In 1770, he left Sweden to continue his studies in natural history in Amsterdam, marking the beginning of a nine-year stay abroad.

During his time in Amsterdam and Leiden in 1771, he visited their botanical gardens and museums, where he met botanists Johannes Burman and his son Nicolaus Laurentius Burman. They were impressed with him and entrusted him with the task of visiting the Dutch colonies and Japan to collect specimens for Dutch botanical gardens. After further studies in medicine in Paris, he returned to the Netherlands. Through these connections, he was able to travel to Japan via South Africa and Java on behalf of the Netherlands in 1772, serving as a surgeon in the service of the Dutch East India Company, as Japan only accepted Dutch citizens. He earned his doctorate in medicine in 1772 with a dissertation on sciatica.

=== South Africa === He left Amsterdam in December 1771, serving as a ship's doctor for the Dutch East India Company. Upon arriving in Cape Town, Cape Colony, he stayed there for three years to learn Dutch and to be able to pass as a Dutchman, since Japan at that time was only open to Protestant Dutch merchants. In September 1772, accompanied by Johan Andreas Auge, the head gardener of the company's garden, he continued north to the natural harbor of Saldanha Bay, traveling east along the Breede Valley through Langkloof Valley all the way to the Gamtoos River and then back across the Karoo plains. Shortly after returning to Cape Town, he met the gardener Francis Masson, the first plant collector for Kew Gardens. They immediately bonded due to their shared interests. During his three expeditions into the interior, Thunberg collected a significant number of specimens of both flora and fauna. Thunberg then sailed to Java in March 1775. He stayed there for two months in Batavia (Jakarta).

=== Japan === In August 1775, he arrived at the Dutch East India Company's trading post on Dejima (1639–1860), a small artificial island in Nagasaki Bay, connected to the city by a single small bridge. He was appointed chief surgeon (1775–1776) at this trading post. However, like the Dutch, he was barely allowed to leave the island. Nevertheless, he became one of the few who were permitted to conduct some botanical research on land. To obtain more specimens, he exchanged his knowledge of European medicine with Japanese interpreters for new samples. These Japanese interpreters were also doctors, like himself, so he also benefited from a medical exchange during his travels. In mid-1776, he was finally allowed to accompany the head of the Dutch settlement to the shogun in Edo (now Tokyo). During this slow journey, he collected many Japanese plants. His scientific work resulted in the first detailed description of the flora and fauna of Japan: "Flora Japonica." Many of the plants that he named with the suffix "japonica" were actually Chinese plants that had been introduced to Japan, and several plants that he described as wild-growing were, in fact, garden plants. In the book *Voyages de C.P. Thunberg, au Japon: par le Cap de Bonne-Espérance, les îles de la Sonde, &c* ("C.P. Thunberg's Journey to Japan, via the Cape of Good Hope, the Sunda Islands, etc."), Thunberg describes his adventurous journey to Japan and his stay there. He paints a bleak picture of life on Dejima in this book. It also includes a short Swedish-Japanese dictionary, and he describes several aspects of daily life in Japan, such as the rule of walking on the left side of the road. Thunberg left Japan in November 1776. He had grown tired of the strict surveillance he was subjected to in Japan and began his journey back to the Netherlands.

=== Return to Sweden === During his return journey, Thunberg, after a brief stay in Java, arrived in Colombo on Ceylon (now Sri Lanka) in July 1777. There, he made several trips, including one to the Dutch settlement of Galle, and collected a large number of plant specimens. Before he could return, he received news that he had been appointed as Carl von Linné the Younger's successor as a botanical demonstrator at Uppsala University. He returned to the Netherlands in 1778 and, after a visit to London, to Sweden in March 1779.

== Academic Career == For nearly fifty years, Thunberg worked as a teacher at Uppsala University. Upon his return, he was appointed demonstrator; in 1781, he became an extraordinary professor, and in 1784, a professor of medicine and botany at Uppsala University, a position he held until...

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the 19th century is very well documented (census records, newspapers, letters). The uncertainty mainly concerns selection and perspective – whose stories are highlighted.
tldr: Carl Peter Thunberg was a Swedish researcher who traveled extensively to study plants and animals in South Africa and Japan. He became known as an important figure in both Japanese and South African natural science.
lattlast:
Carl Peter Thunberg was born in Jönköping in 1743 and studied natural sciences and medicine at Uppsala University. He was a student of the renowned scientist Carl von Linné, who helped him obtain scholarships to study abroad in Amsterdam and Paris. During his travels, he became an expert on plants and animals.

Since Japan only allowed Dutch citizens during the 18th century, Thunberg worked as a doctor for the Dutch East India Company. This enabled him to travel to South Africa and later to Japan. There, he collected various plants and animals to study them in his books. He became very well-known for his work describing the nature of these regions.

Upon returning to Sweden in 1779, he became a professor at Uppsala University. He worked as a teacher of medicine and botany (the study of plants) for almost fifty years. Even today, he is considered a very important figure in natural sciences.
